food_subgroup: Pasta dishes with meat and cream sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for smoked duck pasta; closest parent 'pasta dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific term for smoked duck cream pasta; 'Pasta, cooked' (A00LC) used as closest parent. Smoked duck and cream sauce components are not individually coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 'Pasta (substance)' is the closest available term; no specific smoked duck pasta concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of smoked duck pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a bed of cooked pasta topped with smoked duck slices and cream sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with meat sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with smoked duck breast values from USDA Foundation Foods and cream sauce component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for smoked duck cream pasta exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by component assembly: cooked pasta (USDA SR Legacy NDB 20121, ~158 kcal/100g), smoked duck breast (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171490 duck meat, smoked, ~190 kcal/100g), and cream sauce (heavy cream and butter, USDA SR Legacy). Weighted blend at approximate ratio 50% pasta : 30% smoked duck : 20% cream sauce yields the per-100g composite values reported. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (white, cooked al dente) GI ~45
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for smoked duck cream pasta. GI of 45 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked white pasta (al dente), which has a well-established GI of approximately 45 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). The protein and fat content from smoked duck and cream sauce further attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 29.1, where carbohydrate per 350g serving = 18.5 × 3.5 = 64.75g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~1470 mg per serving) from smoked duck curing and cream sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load (~1470 mg per standard 350g serving), which represents approximately 64% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-salt preparation or consuming a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~29 per serving) from refined wheat pasta, partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the moderate glycemic load (~29 per 350g serving) driven by refined pasta carbohydrates; choosing a smaller portion, requesting wholegrain pasta if available, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~4.9g/100g; ~17g per serving) and sodium from cream sauce and smoked duck
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in smaller portions, as the saturated fat from heavy cream (~17g per serving) and the high sodium from smoked duck curing may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; substituting a lighter sauce and limiting portion size is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Smoked Duck Pasta with Cream Sauce is a Western-style cafe dish popular in Singapore, consisting of al dente pasta (typically fettuccine or linguine) topped with thinly sliced smoked duck breast and a rich, velvety cream sauce made from heavy cream and butter, seasoned with garlic, black pepper, and salt. The smoked duck imparts a distinctive smoky, savory depth to the dish, complementing the mild sweetness of the cream sauce. The texture contrasts tender pasta strands with the slightly firm, smoky duck slices. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~195 kcal/100g), providing moderate protein (~10.5g/100g) from the duck, significant fat (~9.8g/100g) predominantly from the cream sauce, and carbohydrates (~18.5g/100g) from the pasta. Sodium content is notably high (~420 mg/100g) due to the curing process used in smoked duck preparation and sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is low (~45) based on the pasta component, but the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate (~29), relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The saturated fat content (~4.9g/100g) from cream warrants attention for cardiovascular health. This dish is commonly served as a main course in Western-style cafes and casual dining establishments in Singapore, priced around SGD 12–15. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific confirmation from the restaurant.
common_user_queries: How many calories in smoked duck pasta?
Is smoked duck pasta high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat smoked duck pasta?
What is the GI of smoked duck pasta?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional match for smoked duck cream pasta was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by component assembly from USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods data for cooked pasta, smoked duck, and cream sauce at approximate serving ratios. GI is analogously estimated from cooked white pasta GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods; no single approved-source entry for this exact dish was found. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for estimation and is not a primary nutrient of concern for this dish type. 3. The food_name in the input ('Smoked Duck Pasta ⭐') contained a decorative star emoji which has been removed to produce a canonical English food name. 4. The cuisine tag in the input was listed as 'Coffee' (likely referring to the cafe category on GrabFood); the canonical cuisine has been corrected to 'Western' based on the dish's culinary identity. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; smoked duck may not be halal-certified depending on the restaurant. 6. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white pasta (al dente) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component USDA data and carry medium confidence. 8. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 9. This dish is classified as a Singaporean Western cafe dish; multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided per G6 Singaporean dish requirements.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed decorative star emoji from food_name to produce canonical English name.
Corrected cuisine from 'Coffee' (GrabFood category) to 'Western' (culinary identity).
Added mandatory multilingual names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per G6 Singaporean dish rules.
Estimated nutrient values by component assembly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods with full provenance documentation.
Assigned GI of 45 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database cooked white pasta data with full estimation basis documented.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 with explicit note per G3 sentinel value rule.
Corrected a Hindi alias in aliases_by_language from 'स्मोक्ट डक पास्ता' to 'स्मोक्ड डक पास्ता' to ensure accuracy and consistency with the flat aliases list, resolving the Q8 failure.
Ensured all ontology match_type values use only allowed values (exact, closest_parent, none).
Added zero-macro justification not required as no core macro is zero.
Provided specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions.
